canonical_examples.explanation: small-residue block coordinate with remainder k = 1 and quotient q = 27, so the raw coefficients qk^j are the constant sequence 27
canonical_examples.label: Constant-coefficient bridge

canonical_examples.explanation: small-residue block coordinate with q = 1 and remainder k = 3, so the raw coefficients qk^j reduce to literal powers of k and the period is nontrivial
canonical_examples.label: Prime q=1 bridge

canonical_examples.explanation: remainder orbit under multiplication by the base splits by CRT across 3 and 83 while still admitting a readable small-residue block coordinate
canonical_examples.label: Composite periodic core

canonical_examples.explanation: strip base factors to the purely periodic modulus 249; the example shows both preperiod behavior and a q = 1 bridge-style coordinate
canonical_examples.label: Composite with preperiod

canonical_examples.explanation: generator of the QR subgroup appears at stride 2, but the exact QR-generating strides are not all even, making this a useful high-signal counterexample
canonical_examples.label: Prime QR counterexample

carry_dfa.distinctive_feature: in this block coordinate both machines collapse to one observed state, giving the trivial state-relabeling regime

factorization_status.implemented: finite-window output agreement between the carry-propagated block normalization and the long-division DFA
factorization_status.open_boundary: canonical factorization of the entire long-division DFA into orbit plus carry is not established here

carry_dfa.label: Trivial state relabeling

open boundary: canonical factorization of the entire long-division DFA into orbit plus carry is not established here
theorem target: Promote finite-window agreement to a canonical state-level factorization, ideally by a global remainder-to-carry morphism that explains the displayed word for every coprime modulus.

refutation target: Find a bounded example where the displayed words agree on the visible window but even the observed remainder-to-carry quotient candidate fails or becomes incompatible with any simple state relabeling.

weaker replacement: If a canonical factorization fails, downgrade to finite-window output agreement plus an observed quotient-from-remainder-to-carry framework, without claiming a unique global state factorization.

carry_selector.counterexample_target: Use this case to test naive claims that larger block widths, stripped cores, or output agreement alone determine the state-level regime.
carry_selector.explanation: The selector profile finite_word_only -> state_relabeling -> finite_word_only shows that trivial relabeling can appear only at one isolated block width.
carry_selector.heuristic_note: Selector profiles appear to capture stable family behavior rather than just finding the smallest residue coordinate.
carry_selector.label: Isolated k = 1 relabeling window
carry_selector.theorem_candidate: Classify when selector profiles admit isolated or repeated state-relabeling windows beyond the trivial k = 1 coordinates.

carry_selector.counterexample_target: Use this case to test naive claims that larger block widths, stripped cores, or output agreement alone determine the state-level regime.
carry_selector.explanation: This is the cleanest decimal example where the selector chooses a genuine non-k = 1 relabeling window, at m = 6 with k = 16.
carry_selector.heuristic_note: Selector profiles appear to capture stable family behavior rather than just finding the smallest residue coordinate.
carry_selector.label: Isolated non-k = 1 relabeling window
carry_selector.theorem_candidate: Classify when selector profiles admit isolated or repeated state-relabeling windows beyond the trivial k = 1 coordinates.

carry_selector.counterexample_target: Use this case to test naive claims that larger block widths, stripped cores, or output agreement alone determine the state-level regime.
carry_selector.explanation: The same-core denominator 996 never reaches a relabeling window on the tested profile, even though 249 does.
carry_selector.heuristic_note: Selector profiles appear to capture stable family behavior rather than just finding the smallest residue coordinate.
carry_selector.label: Same-core quotient-only persistence
carry_selector.theorem_candidate: Classify when selector profiles admit isolated or repeated state-relabeling windows beyond the trivial k = 1 coordinates.

carry_selector_families.counterexample_target: Refute any claim that state relabeling can only occur at trivial constant-coefficient or repunit-style coordinates.
carry_selector_families.explanation: State-relabeling is not confined to k = 1 coordinates. The selector isolates nontrivial relabeling windows at examples like 17, 19, 29, and 249 in base 10.
carry_selector_families.heuristic_note: Non-k = 1 relabeling windows appear sparse and often isolated in m, which makes them plausible theorem targets rather than random noise.
carry_selector_families.label: Non-k = 1 relabeling windows
carry_selector_families.theorem_candidate: Determine arithmetic criteria for non-k = 1 selector windows where the observed carry and remainder states become bijective on the visible trace.

carry_selector_families.counterexample_target: Use same-core families to reject any theorem candidate that ignores actual-denominator effects once the periodic core is fixed.
carry_selector_families.explanation: The selector profile is not determined by stripped periodic core. Core 249 supports an isolated relabeling window, while same-core 498 and 996 stay quotient-only.
carry_selector_families.heuristic_note: The selector profile depends on the actual denominator, not just the periodic core and not just the visible output word.
carry_selector_families.label: Same-core relabeling loss
carry_selector_families.theorem_candidate: Classify which same-core deformations preserve, shift, or destroy relabeling windows.

carry_selector_families.counterexample_target: Reject any monotonicity rule that predicts relabeling windows only move in one direction or can only disappear.
carry_selector_families.explanation: Small-multiple moves can preserve and enlarge relabeling windows rather than merely shifting or destroying them: 17 relabels at m = 5, 34 relabels at m = 5 and 7, and 68 loses relabeling entirely.
carry_selector_families.heuristic_note: Selector profiles behave like family objects under small multiples, not like single-coordinate accidents.
carry_selector_families.label: Small-multiple relabeling shift and enlargement
carry_selector_families.theorem_candidate: Describe how multiplying by stripped base factors changes selector signatures and relabeling-mode sets.

composite_examples.coordinate_summary: actual coordinate m = 6, q = 47619, k = 1; periodic-core coordinate m = 6, q = 47619, k = 1
composite_examples.explanation: two CRT components with local orders 1 and 6, so ord_M(base) = lambda(M) = 6; the small-residue block coordinate has k = 1, so the carry layer stays trivial
composite_examples.label: CRT split with exact lambda

composite_examples.coordinate_summary: actual coordinate m = 3, q = 37, k = 1; periodic-core coordinate m = 3, q = 37, k = 1
composite_examples.explanation: prime-power example where ord_M(base) = 3 is a strict divisor of lambda(M) = 18, showing that the Carmichael value is an upper bound rather than an equality in general
composite_examples.label: Prime-power strict Carmichael bound

composite_examples.coordinate_summary: actual coordinate m = 3, q = 4, k = 4; periodic-core coordinate m = 3, q = 4, k = 4
composite_examples.explanation: purely periodic composite core with components 3 and 83; the 83-component carries the full period 41, while the small-residue block coordinate is 10^3 = 4*249 + 4
composite_examples.label: Composite periodic core

composite_examples.coordinate_summary: actual coordinate m = 3, q = 1, k = 4; periodic-core coordinate m = 3, q = 4, k = 4
composite_examples.explanation: same stripped periodic core as 249, but with a two-digit preperiod from the removed factor 2^2; the actual denominator still admits the bridge-style coordinate 10^3 = 1*996 + 4
composite_examples.label: Preperiod plus periodic core

composite_families.carry_focus: carry stays secondary here; the main phenomenon is local order growth versus the Carmichael bound
composite_families.coordinate_focus: small-residue coordinates exist, but the family is primarily about order lifting across 3, 9, 27, 81
composite_families.crt_focus: single-component prime-power family where local order can stabilize before growing
composite_families.explanation: This family shows a genuinely composite phenomenon: local orders on prime powers do not simply copy the prime case. For base 10, ord_3(10) = ord_9(10) = 1, then the order grows to 3 on 27 and 9 on 81.
composite_families.label: Prime-power lifting at p = 3
composite_families.preperiod_focus: no stripped base factors appear because every member is coprime to the base

composite_families.carry_focus: 249 has 7 carry states while 996 has 5; the carry layer changes, but the periodic core does not
composite_families.crt_focus: both examples share the same CRT local orders because the purely periodic modulus is 249 in both cases
composite_families.explanation: This family separates the purely periodic remainder-orbit structure from the base-factor preperiod. The pair is the clearest composite example where CRT data, preperiod length, and carry normalization can be discussed independently.
composite_families.label: Shared periodic core 249 / 996
composite_families.preperiod_focus: only 996 has a preperiod, coming from the stripped factor 2^2

visibility.counterexample_target: Use this case family to test and refute naive visibility rules based only on local overflow.
visibility.explanation: q is large but k = 1, so every requested raw block is already legal and no incoming carry appears.
visibility.heuristic_note: Small k still correlates with longer readable raw prefixes, but the exact boundary is not yet a theorem.
visibility.label: Carry-free constant window
summary_lines: block width m = 6, block base B = 1000000, q = 47619, k = 1

implemented relation: agreement length = min(local overflow boundary, incoming-carry boundary)

lookahead certificate matches on this case = True

visibility_families.counterexample_target: Rule out any visibility criterion that ignores preperiod data once the same q = 1 residue pattern appears in both pure and preperiodic settings.
visibility_families.explanation: Both cases have q = 1 and small k, but one is prime-purely-periodic and the other has a decimal preperiod. Compare raw-prefix agreement length and lookahead rather than only k.
visibility_families.heuristic_note: q = 1 often gives unusually readable early blocks, but 996 shows that preperiod and tail carry still matter.
visibility_families.label: q = 1 carried-prefix family
visibility_families.theorem_candidate: Use the exact incoming-carry boundary as a base case for proving lookahead bounds in the q = 1 bridge regime.

visibility_families.counterexample_target: Refute any criterion that treats q > 1 as automatically unreadable.
visibility_families.explanation: These cases separate positive-q constant windows from positive-q early-carry intrusion, so q > 1 by itself is not a visibility obstruction.
visibility_families.heuristic_note: Large q can still be perfectly legible when k = 1, while moderate q with k > 1 can fail early.
visibility_families.label: q > 1 carried-prefix family
visibility_families.theorem_candidate: Determine how q interacts with k and B in quantitative lookahead bounds once the incoming-carry boundary is fixed exactly.

visibility_families.counterexample_target: Test any proposed same-core formula on both the exact-power case 996/249 and the non-power case 498/249.
visibility_families.explanation: This family isolates one periodic core while varying the stripped base-factor ratio. The comparison 996/249 shows the exact k-power shift law, while 498/249 shows the broader two-point interval law when the q-ratio is not itself a power of k.
visibility_families.heuristic_note: The periodic core strongly constrains k and B-k, but lookahead and preperiod behavior still depend on the actual denominator.
visibility_families.label: Shared periodic core with different preperiods
summary_lines: shared B = 1000, shared k = 4, shared B-k = 996
threshold shift interval law holds = True
exact k-power shift law holds = True
shared B = 1000, shared k = 4, shared B-k = 996
q_ratio as power of k = None
threshold shift interval law holds = True
exact k-power shift law holds = False
visibility_families.theorem_candidate: Classify same-core families by q-ratio intervals between consecutive powers of k, and separate which observables satisfy exact shift laws versus interval laws.

visibility_families.counterexample_target: Test whether any claimed exact same-core law survives after changing base while preserving the q-ratio power relation.
visibility_families.explanation: Base 7 supplies a non-decimal exact-law family: in the shared coordinate B = 343 and k = 7, the pair 56/8 realizes the same one-block exact shift seen in the decimal 996/249 family.
visibility_families.heuristic_note: Exact k-power shift laws appear across bases once a shared coordinate with small k is fixed.
visibility_families.label: Cross-base same-core exact law
summary_lines: shared B = 343, shared k = 7, shared B-k = 336
threshold shift interval law holds = True
exact k-power shift law holds = True
visibility_families.theorem_candidate: Show which same-core exact-shift laws depend only on the shared q-ratio power relation and not on the ambient base.

visibility_families.counterexample_target: Reject any family claim that only tests one endpoint of the interval law or only exact k-power ratios.
visibility_families.explanation: Base 12 exhibits all three local same-core outcomes in one shared coordinate B = 144, k = 4: 10/5 realizes the lower interval endpoint, 70/35 realizes the upper interval endpoint, and 20/5 realizes the exact k-power law.
visibility_families.heuristic_note: A single shared coordinate can realize lower-endpoint, upper-endpoint, and exact-shift behavior across different actual/core pairs.
visibility_families.label: Cross-base interval endpoints in one coordinate
